revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Confucian Shinto]][[fr:Juka shintō]][[ja:儒家神道]][[simple:Confucian Shinto]][[zh:儒家神道]]
{{Short description|Syncretic religious tradition in Japan|qq=}} [[File:Razan_Hayashi.jpg|link=https://ja.Wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%95%E3%82%A1%E3%82%A4%E3%83%AB:Razan_Hayashi.jpg|right|thumb|257x257px|{{ill|Hayashi Razan|en|Hayashi Razan|qid=Q558279|lt=Razan Hayashi|1=Hayashi Razan}}]] '''Confucian Shinto''', also known as {{Nihongo|2=儒家神道|3='''Juka Shintō'''|qq=}} in {{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|qid=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}}, is a syncretic religious tradition that combines elements of {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|qid=Q9581|lt=Confucianism|1=Confucianism}} and [[Shinto]].<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Perspectives toward Understanding the Concept of Kami |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/cpjr/kami/intro.html |access-date=2023-03-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp|qq=}}</ref><ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9912 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja|qq=}}</ref> It originated in Japan during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} (1603–1868), and is sometimes referred to as "{{ill|Edo neo-Confucianism|en|Edo neo-Confucianism|qid=Q5340141|lt=Neo-Confucian|1=Edo neo-Confucianism}} Shinto"<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Kun-chiang |first=Chang |date=2018-10-02 |title=Neo-Confucian Shinto Thought in Early Tokugawa Zhu Xi Studies: Comparing the Work of Hayashi Razan and Yamazaki Ansai |url=https://doi.org/10.1080/10971467.2018.1654823 |journal=Contemporary Chinese Thought |volume=49 |issue=3-4 |pages=219–240 |doi=10.1080/10971467.2018.1654823 |issn=1097-1467|qq=}}</ref> Modern organizations include {{ill|Shintō Taiseikyō|en|Shintō Taiseikyō|qid=Q111992480|lt=Shinto Taiseikyo|1=Shintō Taiseikyō}},<ref name="caroline-myss-2023a">{{Cite web |title=The Forms of Shinto |url=https://www.myss.com/free-resources/world-religions/shinto/the-forms-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-03-06 |website=Caroline Myss |language=en-US|qq=}}</ref><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Kyōha Shintō {{!|qq=}} Japanese religion {{!|qq=}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Kyoha-Shinto |access-date=2023-03-06 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en|qq=}}</ref> {{ill|Shinto Shusei|en|Shinto Shusei|qid=Q11590921|lt=Shinto Shusei|1=Shintō Shūsei-ha}},<ref name="caroline-myss-2023a" /><ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023a" /> and [[Tensha Tsuchimikado Shinto|Tsuchimikado Shinto]].<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023b">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8943 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja|qq=}}</ref> [[Suika Shinto]] was a major school of Confucian Shinto.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023b">{{Cite web |title=Japan - Shintō and kokugaku {{!|qq=}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/place/Japan/Shinto-and-kokugaku |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en|qq=}}</ref> == History == The roots of Confucian Shinto can be traced back to the 17th century, when Japanese scholars began to study Chinese Confucian texts and integrate Confucian ideas into their understanding of Shinto. The most notable of these scholars was {{ill|Yamazaki Ansai|en|Yamazaki Ansai|qid=Q6565636|lt=Yamazaki Ansai|1=Yamazaki Ansai}} (1618–1682), who developed a synthesis of Confucianism and Shinto that emphasized the importance of ethical behavior and filial piety.<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" /> Ansai's teachings were further developed by his disciple, [[Kaibara Ekken]] (1630–1714),<ref>{{cite book |last=Josephson |first=Jason Ā. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C |title=The Invention of Religion in Japan |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=2012 |isbn=9780226412351 |location=Chicago |pages=115–6|qq=}}</ref> who wrote a number of influential works on Confucian Shinto, including the "Greater Learning for Women."<ref>[http://chnm.gmu.edu/wwh/p/84.html See excerpt] (hosted by George Mason University)</ref> Ekken's writings emphasized the importance of education and the cultivation of virtue, and advocated for the integration of Confucian ethics into everyday life.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kaibara Ekken {{!|qq=}} Encyclopedia.com |url=https://www.encyclopedia.com/history/historians-and-chronicles/historians-miscellaneous-biographies/kaibara-ekken |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.encyclopedia.com|qq=}}</ref> In the 18th and 19th centuries, Confucian Shinto became increasingly popular among the {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} class, who saw it as a way to reconcile their duty to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}} with their Confucian ideals of loyalty, honor, and righteousness.<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" /> Confucian Shinto also gained a foothold among commoners, who were attracted to its emphasis on {{ill|Family values|en|Family values|qid=Q1811480|lt=Family values|1=Family values}} and {{ill|Applied ethics|en|Applied ethics|qid=Q538733|lt=practical ethics|1=Applied ethics}}.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023a" /> {{ill|Wang Yangming|en|Wang Yangming|qid=Q378462|lt=Wang Yangming's|1=Wang Yangming}} concept of {{ill|Unity of knowledge and action|en|Unity of knowledge and action|qid=Q15933119|lt=union of knowledge and practice|1=Unity of knowledge and action}} was highly influential on Confucian Shinto.<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" /> == Influence and legacy == Confucian Shinto had a significant impact on Japanese society during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} and beyond. It helped to shape the moral values and social norms of the samurai class,<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" /> and played a role in the development of modern Japanese nationalism.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023b" /> Confucian Shinto also had an influence on the [[Meiji Restoration]] of 1868, which marked the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} and the beginning of [[:en:Modern Japan|Modern Japan]]. The leaders of the restoration sought to revive traditional {{ill|Japanese values|en|Japanese values|qid=Q6159142|lt=Japanese values|1=Japanese values}} and {{ill|Culture of Japan|en|Culture of Japan|qid=Q831454|lt=culture|1=Culture of Japan}}, and saw Confucian Shinto as a way to do so. They promoted the idea of [[kokutai]], or the "national essence", which was based on the Confucian notion of the ruler-subject relationship and the Shinto idea of the emperor as a divine being.<ref name="wwwbritannicacom-2023b" /> [[:en:Fukkoshinto|Fukko Shinto]] which was also quite significant developed in reaction to Confucian Shinto,<ref name="yougo13">{{Cite book |last=全国歴史教育研究協議会 |title=日本史B用語集―A併記 |publisher=[[山川出版社]] |isbn=9784634013025 |edition=改訂版 |series= |author-link= |access-date= |orig-date=2009-03-30|qq=}}</ref><ref name="kokushi321">[[:ja:平重道|平重道]]「儒家神道」『[[:ja:国史大辞典_(昭和時代)|国史大辞典]]』第7巻、[[:ja:吉川弘文館|吉川弘文館]]、1986th year [[:ja:特別:文献資料/4642005072|ISBN 4-642-00507-2]] Page 321</ref> and it was the predecessor to {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}.<ref>{{Cite book |last=日本史用語研究会 |title=必携日本史用語 |publisher=[[実教出版]] |isbn=9784407316599 |edition=四訂版 |series= |author-link= |access-date=|qq=}}</ref> == Modern groups == Among [[Sect Shinto]], {{Nihongo|[[:en:Shinto Taiseikyo|Shinto Taiseikyo]]|神道大成教|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinto Shusei|en|Shinto Shusei|qid=Q11590921|lt=Shinto Shusei|1=Shintō Shūsei-ha}} are considered Confucian influenced groups.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kyōha Shintō {{!|qq=}} Japanese religion |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Kyoha-Shinto |access-date=2023-03-06 |website=Encyclopedia Britannica |language=en|qq=}}</ref> [[Tensha Tsuchimikado Shinto|Tsuchimikado Shinto]] continues the legacy as well,<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2023b" /> but it is not a member of the sect Shinto association. == References == {{Reflist|qq=}} {{Religious Confucianism|qq=}}
[[en:Confucian Shinto]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[it:Shintoismo confuciano]] [[ja:儒家神道]] [[simple:Confucian Shinto]] [[zh:儒家神道]] {{wikidata link|Q18337053}} [[Category:Religious Confucianism]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Confucianism in Japan]] [[Category:江戸時代の神道]] [[Category:Shinto schools of thought]]
